metadata.title: The Business Case: Model, GTM, Competition + Ask (Slides 5–7, 10)
metadata.playlist: Sell the Vision — Your Investor Pitch Video
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 brief — Video Pitch to Investors: Slides 5–7, 10 (business model, GTM, competition, ask)

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 4 of 7. This is where a cool project becomes a fundable business. Most students under-build these four slides — business model, go-to-market, competition, and the ask. They write two sentences on each and move on. That is the mistake. These four slides together are what separate a project presentation from an investor pitch. They show that you have thought about money, customers, rivals, and what you are asking for. Investors fund businesses, not demos. If you can answer 'how do you make money,' 'who are your first ten customers and how do you get them,' 'who else is in this space and why do you win,' and 'what do you want from us' — that is a business case. Episode 4 covers all four: slides 5, 6, 7, and 10.

props.segment: The Business Case: Model, GTM, Competition + Ask (Slides 5–7, 10)
props.command: Write slides 5–7 and 10: business model with labeled revenue estimate, first-10-customers GTM, competition with unfair advantage, and a specific ask with timeline.
props.runningText: these four slides separate a project presentation from an investor pitch — money, customers, rivals, and the ask…
output: Slide 5: pricing model · labeled revenue estimate (subscription/one-time/freemium)
Slide 6: first 10 customers specifically — channels, tactics, timeline
Slide 7: competition · why you win · unfair advantage (reuse A6 positioning)
Slide 10: the ask — amount, use of funds, timeline, clear call to action

beats.narration_text: Slide 5 is the business model. Its job is to answer: how do you make money? Three options are the most common for a tool like yours: subscription — a monthly or annual fee per seat; one-time — pay once, use forever; or freemium — a free tier plus a paid pro tier. Pick the one that matches how the Part 2A user actually buys software. Email marketing coordinators at agencies are accustomed to monthly SaaS subscriptions. That is probably your model. Once you have the model, put a rough revenue estimate on the slide. It does not have to be precise — it needs to exist. '$50 per month per user. If we reach 100 paying users in year one, that is $5,000 in monthly recurring revenue.' Label it as an estimate. That is a business model slide. What the grader is looking for: a pricing mechanism exists, the revenue arithmetic is shown, and the numbers are labeled. A pitch with no monetization slide is asking the investor to assume you have thought about money. Do not make them assume. Show your work.

props.artifactHeading: How you make money — pricing + labeled revenue estimate
artifactLines: Pick a model: subscription (monthly/annual) · one-time purchase · freemium (free + paid pro).
Match the model to how your Part 2A user actually buys software.
Show the arithmetic: '$50/month × 100 users = $5k MRR in year one.' Label as estimate.
Grader check: pricing mechanism + revenue math + labeled numbers. All three must be present.
No monetization slide = asking the investor to assume you thought about money. Show your work.
props.sparkLine: Rough + labeled beats precise + uncited. Show the arithmetic.

beats.narration_text: Slide 6 is go-to-market. Its job is to answer: how do you get your first 10 customers? Not your first 1,000 — your first 10. The reason you start with 10 is that investors know most founders have not thought past 'we'll post on social media,' and they want to see that you have a real acquisition plan. A real first-10-customers plan names specific channels. 'We will post in three email marketing Facebook groups and offer a free two-week trial — we estimate this will generate 20 signups, of which 5 become paying.' That is a GTM. 'We will do social media marketing' is not. Specific channels for a B2B tool like this one: LinkedIn posts targeting agency owners, outreach to email marketing associations, a post in the n8n community forum, a post in a relevant Slack or Discord group, a cold email to 20 agency contacts from LinkedIn Sales Navigator. Use Claude to brainstorm the right channels for your specific tool and user. The question is: where does your Part 2A user actually spend their time online?

props.artifactHeading: How you get your first 10 customers — specifically
artifactLines: Target: first 10 customers, not first 1,000. Specificity is the signal investors look for.
Wrong: 'we'll do social media marketing.' Right: named channels + estimated conversion.
Example: '3 email-marketing Facebook groups · free 2-week trial · estimate: 20 signups, 5 paying.'
B2B channels: LinkedIn targeting → relevant Slack/Discord groups → community forums → cold outreach.
Key question: where does your Part 2A user actually spend time online? That's your channel.
props.sparkLine: Name the channels. Estimate the conversion. Show you know where your user is.

beats.narration_text: Slide 7 is the competition slide. Its job is to show that you know who else exists, and to make the investor believe you win. Every investor knows you have competitors. If you say 'there is no competition,' you either have not done the research or you are describing a problem no one has — which means it might not be a problem worth solving. The format: name two or three direct competitors and one indirect competitor, then state your unfair advantage. Reuse the positioning work you did in Assignment 6. The competitive analysis you built there — the matrix, the differentiator, the one thing — maps directly onto this slide. 'Competitor A does X but not Y. Competitor B serves enterprise, not SMB. We are the only one that does Z specifically for W.' That is a competition slide. The unfair advantage should be something a direct competitor could not copy in a week. If your advantage is 'we use AI,' that is not an advantage — every competitor uses AI. If your advantage is 'we are the only tool built specifically for agencies with fewer than 50 clients who use HubSpot,' that is specific and defensible.

props.artifactHeading: Who else exists · why you win · unfair advantage
artifactLines: 'No competition' = red flag. Every investor knows you have competitors.
Format: 2–3 direct + 1 indirect → then your unfair advantage.
Reuse A6 positioning: the differentiator and the one thing map directly here.
Weak advantage: 'we use AI.' Every competitor uses AI.
Strong advantage: 'the only tool built for agencies under 50 clients using HubSpot' — specific, defensible.
Test: could a direct competitor copy your advantage in a week? If yes, find a different one.
props.sparkLine: 'We use AI' is not an advantage. Be specific and defensible.

beats.narration_text: Slide 10 is the ask — the close. Its job is to answer: what do you want, from whom, and by when? An ask has three components. The amount or resource: how much funding, or what kind of help — mentorship, introductions, early customers, technical advisors. The use of funds: what specifically you will do with it. 'Hire a part-time developer for six months and run $2,000 in paid LinkedIn ads.' Not 'grow the business' — something specific. And a timeline with a milestone: 'We will have 50 paying customers by month four.' No clear ask means no clear close. Investors cannot say yes if they do not know what you want. The two most common mistakes: no ask at all — students end on 'that's our pitch' with no call to action; and a vague ask — 'we are looking for investment' does not tell the investor an amount, a use, or a timeline. Make the ask specific, direct, and realistic. A $50,000 ask to build an MVP is reasonable. A $5 million ask from a class project is not.

props.artifactHeading: What you want · use of funds · timeline
artifactLines: 3 parts: amount/resource · specific use of funds · timeline with a milestone.
Example: '$50k → hire part-time dev + $2k LinkedIn ads → 50 paying customers by month 4.'
No ask = no close. Investors can't say yes if they don't know what you want.
Trap 1: no ask at all — ending on 'that's our pitch' with no call to action.
Trap 2: 'we are looking for investment' — no amount, no use, no timeline. Not an ask.
Reality check: $50k for an MVP = reasonable. $5M from a class project = not.
props.sparkLine: Amount · use · timeline. Make the ask specific and realistic.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Episode 4. Four slides that separate a project presentation from a fundable pitch. Slide 5: pricing mechanism exists, revenue arithmetic is shown, numbers are labeled. Slide 6: first 10 customers specifically named — channels, estimated conversion, where the user spends time. Slide 7: two or three named competitors, unfair advantage that a direct competitor could not copy in a week. Reuse your A6 positioning work. Slide 10: amount or resource, specific use of funds, timeline with a milestone. No vague asks. The full business case across these four slides tells the investor: this person has thought about money, customers, rivals, and what they need. That is the difference between a demo and a business.

props.artifactTitle: The Business Case: Model, GTM, Competition + Ask
artifactLines: Slide 5: pricing + revenue arithmetic + labeled estimates. No monetization = missing slide.
Slide 6: first 10 customers specifically — named channels + estimated conversion.
Slide 7: named competitors + unfair advantage that a competitor can't copy in a week.
Slide 10: amount/resource · specific use of funds · timeline with milestone.
These 4 slides say: this person has thought about money, customers, rivals, and the ask.
That's the difference between a demo and a business.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude. Fill in the brackets before running. The prompt reads: Here is my Madison project: [paste the one-liner and the Part 2A user]. Write slides 5, 6, 7, and 10 of my investor pitch. Slide 5: write a business model with one of these formats — subscription, one-time, or freemium — and include a rough labeled revenue estimate using the formula: price × estimated users × period. Slide 6: write a first-10-customers go-to-market plan. Name at least three specific channels where the Part 2A user spends time online. Estimate the conversion from reach to signup to paying customer. Slide 7: name two direct competitors and one indirect competitor. For each, write what they do well and why they fall short for this specific user. Then write the unfair advantage — something a direct competitor could not copy in a week. Pull from the A6 positioning if available: [paste A6 differentiator if you have it]. Slide 10: write the ask — the funding amount or resource, what it will be used for specifically, and the milestone you will hit by month four. After the prompt runs: does each slide answer its one question? Does the competition slide name a real unfair advantage?

props.segment: The Business Case: Model, GTM, Competition + Ask (Slides 5–7, 10)
props.command: Here is my Madison project: [one-liner + Part 2A user]. Write Slides 5, 6, 7, and 10. S5: pricing model (sub/one-time/freemium) + rough labeled revenue estimate (price × users × period). S6: first-10-customers GTM — 3 specific channels, estimated conversion. S7: 2 direct + 1 indirect competitor, unfair advantage a competitor can't copy in a week [paste A6 differentiator if available]. S10: ask — amount/resource · specific use · month-4 milestone.
props.runningText: paste your one-liner, Part 2A user, and A6 differentiator before running — the business case must be specific to your product and user…

beats.narration_text: The Business Case: Model, GTM, Competition + Ask. Liam, in for Bear.
